top_strategic.title: The Brand's Own Idea Lab (White Label)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Sell Nike their own instance of our Idea Lab analyzing ONLY clips where their products appear. Nike uploads competitor videos, our system finds gaps: 'Your shoes underrepresented in backcourt angles' or 'Opponent's racket featured 3x more in viral moments.' Nike pays $200K/year, gets insights they can't get anywhere else, and becomes dependent on our data infrastructure.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Nike already has armies of sports marketing analysts and competitor tracking tools; they won't pay Pickle DaaS $50K+/year to repackage their own video library back to them.
counterargument.evidence: This assumes brands have a blind spot for their own product visibility—they don't. Nike's marketing team watches their own clips constantly and likely already uses Vision AI, Hookit, or internal tools for competitor benchmarking. More critically, you'd be asking them to hand over proprietary competitor videos to an unproven startup, creating compliance and legal risk on their end. The willingness-to-pay is near zero because the problem isn't unsolved; it's already solved by bigger, trusted vendors.

top_strategic.title: Brand Bidding Arena
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Flip the model: brands pay to COMPETE for shelf space in our clips. Instead of us detecting Nike in the background, Nike, JOOLA, and On bid in real-time for product placement in future Courtana videos. Facility owners get a cut. The highest bidder's gear gets featured in the next match at that court.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Real-time bidding for product placement in live or near-live clips requires supply (Courtana's video volume) to be predictable and brands to commit ad spend on speculative exposure.
counterargument.evidence: Courtana's clip volume is inconsistent (depends on tournament schedule). Brands won't bid on uncertain inventory. More critically, this model requires Courtana to *actively control* which products appear in clips—either by directing camera angles (unethical, undermines content credibility) or by post-processing (illegal product insertion). The model collapses if you can't guarantee placement. You'd also face immediate pushback from venue partners and athletes who won't tolerate their content being auctioned to the highest bidder.

top_strategic.title: The Idea Lab as SaaS for Any Sports Facility Network
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Package the Idea Lab as a $5K/month white-label product for regional sports facility chains (not just pickleball). A chain with 20 multi-sport courts gets an Idea Lab analyzing all their footage autonomously. We become the hidden OS for facility intelligence. Each facility pays us; we own the data; we expand to basketball, tennis, etc.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Regional sports facility chains won't pay $5K/month for an AI that tells them what equipment brands appear in their footage—they care about facility operations, member retention, and court booking, not brand analytics.
counterargument.evidence: You're selling a feature (brand detection) as a product to a customer (facility chains) who don't have a budget line item for brand intelligence. The buyer at a 20-court facility is the operations manager, not a marketing executive. Even if they see value, you have no distribution into this market, no existing relationships, and would need a 12-18 month sales cycle per facility. Meanwhile, you're stretched thin building this while supporting Courtana.

top_strategic.title: Facility as Media Studio (Revenue Share)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Stop selling clips. Partner with facilities to become mini ESPN studios. We handle all editing/commentary/distribution; they keep 70% of ad revenue + sponsorship deals we broker. A facility with Courtana becomes a media asset. We scale to 100 facilities = 100 revenue streams. Each facility is incentivized to host bigger tournaments.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Facilities won't accept a 30% revenue cut and the operational overhead of becoming a 'media studio' for an unproven ad revenue share model.
counterargument.evidence: Facilities' core business is court rentals and memberships—predictable, recurring revenue. Asking them to bet on ad revenue from edited clips means cannibalizing their existing sponsorship deals (local, direct) and trusting Pickle DaaS to actually broker deals and deliver payments. You'd be taking operational risk (editing, distribution, rights management) that facilities can't control. Also: if a clip goes viral and creates liability (injury, discrimination, etc.), who owns the legal risk? This partnership structure is unresolved and will kill deals in legal review.

top_strategic.title: Coaching Certification Authority
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Use our AI to certify pickleball coaches. Coaches submit videos of their instruction; we analyze shot breakdown, communication clarity, drill design. Certified coaches get a badge, can charge 3x more, and must use Courtana courts to maintain certification. We become the permissioning layer for professional coaching.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Coaching certification requires legal liability framework, accreditation body status, and recurring instructor education—none of which Pickle DaaS is equipped to provide or defend in court.
counterargument.evidence: You cannot simply issue badges that claim to 'certify' coaches. Coaches will charge 3x more based on your certification, and if an injury occurs during a lesson from a 'certified' coach, you're exposed to negligence liability. You'd need insurance, a legal framework, appeals process, and continuing education requirements. This isn't a software feature—it's a regulated credential business. The PPA or IPTPA already own this space. You'd face immediate cease-and-desist letters, and no insurance company will cover a startup playing credential issuer.

top_data.title: DaaS badge prediction: F1=0.97 (precision=0.98, recall=0.96)
top_data.evidence_summary: Across 287 verified clips: 999 correct predictions, 16 false positives, 41 missed badges. Overall F1=0.972.
top_data.recommended_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the ROI of fixing this? How many clips affected?
follow_up.proposed_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

counterargument.argument: An F1=0.97 on 287 clips doesn't prove the model works on unseen badge types or real-world production footage with different lighting, angles, or video quality.
counterargument.evidence: The 287 verified clips likely come from controlled production environments where Courtana already knows what badges exist. When the model encounters a new badge type, variation in video quality, or edge cases from user-generated content, performance will crater. The 16 false positives and 41 missed badges (out of ~1,300 total predictions) suggest systematic blindspots that prompt engineering alone won't fix—you're likely overfitting to known badge patterns.

top_data.title: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add positive examples
top_data.evidence_summary: Production awards these but DaaS misses them: 'rally master' (6 missed), 'new look' (5 missed), 'steady eddie' (4 missed), 'fresh fit' (3 missed), 'paddle tap celebration' (3 missed). These are real badges that our prompt doesn't detect.
top_data.recommended_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]

follow_up.follow_up_question: Can we A/B test this prompt change on 50 clips?
follow_up.proposed_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]

top_data.title: Net/venue branding inflates 1 brands — true share-of-court differs from raw counts
top_data.evidence_summary: Brands with significant venue-only detections (not product use): JOOLA (195/1931 are venue). Raw counts overstate market share. Product-use only: JOOLA=1736, Nike=198, PICKLEBALL=77, HEAD=45, PICKLEBALL KINGDOM=24.
top_data.recommended_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

follow_up.follow_up_question: Is the 'Net/venue branding inflates 1 brands — true share-' signal statistically significant with larger corpus?
follow_up.proposed_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

counterargument.argument: Correcting for venue-only detections assumes brands care about 'true share-of-court'—but they actually care about total visibility, which includes venue branding.
counterargument.evidence: Your brand partners (Nike, JOOLA) are paying for *impressions*, not just product-in-hand moments. A jersey or court net with a brand logo is still brand exposure worth money. By filtering out venue detections, you're actually reducing the value metric you're selling to them. If you report only 'product-use' metrics, you'll undercount Nike's true exposure (venue + gear) relative to competitors, and they'll ask why they're paying for an incomplete picture. You can't have it both ways: either total visibility matters (and venue counts) or you need to price models differently for each metric.

top_data.title: Viral formula: quality (7.59 vs 5.42) + watchability (6.45 vs 3.14)
top_data.evidence_summary: Top 100 viral clips: quality=7.59, watchability=6.45. Bottom 100: quality=5.42, watchability=3.14. Both metrics track strongly with viral potential.
top_data.recommended_action: Use (quality + watchability) / 2 as quick viral prediction score

follow_up.follow_up_question: Can we build a predictive model from this signal?
follow_up.proposed_action: Use (quality + watchability) / 2 as quick viral prediction score

top_data.title: Worst predicted badges: serve returner (F1=0.875), hustler (F1=0.769)
top_data.evidence_summary: Lowest F1 badges: serve returner (F1=0.875), hustler (F1=0.769). These need prompt improvement.
top_data.recommended_action: Add specific examples for these badge types in Gemini prompt

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the ROI of fixing this? How many clips affected?
follow_up.proposed_action: Add specific examples for these badge types in Gemini prompt

top_20.title: Prompt fix: JOOLA appears in 215/287 verified clips — add disambiguation
top_20.evidence_summary: JOOLA dominates 75% of clips. Prompt should distinguish: paddle logo (real JOOLA use), net logo (JOOLA sponsors nets), and venue branding (JOOLA sponsors the facility).
top_20.recommended_action:
Add to brand detection prompt:
'For JOOLA specifically, classify detection source:
  - paddle: JOOLA logo on paddle face = confirmed product use
  - net: JOOLA logo on net = venue sponsorship, NOT product use
  - venue: JOOLA on walls/banners = facility sponsorship
Only count paddle detections as brand_category: paddle'

top_20.title: Sponsorship whitespace detected in 973 clips (88.5%) — sell to brands
top_20.evidence_summary: We detect unbranded equipment/space in 973 clips. This is the gap between what's visible and what's branded — sellable data.
top_20.recommended_action: Package sponsorship whitespace data as 'brand opportunity report' for equipment companies

source_clips.summary: A 17-shot driving rally ends with an unforced error into the net.
Players engage in a prolonged kitchen dink rally, demonstrating patience and control.
Players engage in a consistent rally of dinks at the kitchen line.

top_20.title: Viral shot: 'overhead' clips average 5.8 viral vs 'serve' at 1.25
top_20.evidence_summary: Shot viral averages: overhead=5.8, volley=4.19, dink=3.22, drive=3.09, serve=1.25
top_20.recommended_action: Prioritize highlight reels featuring high-viral shot types

top_20.title: Viral arc: 'top_play' is 130.0x more likely in top clips (13.0% vs 0.0%)
top_20.evidence_summary: Story arc 'top_play' appears in 13.0% of top-viral clips but only 0.0% of bottom-viral clips.
top_20.recommended_action: Prioritize 'top_play' clips in automated highlight reels

top_20.title: Endorsement intelligence: 61 player-brand pairs with 3+ co-occurrences
top_20.evidence_summary: Top pairs: PickleBill-JOOLA(223x), Tmf-JOOLA(145x), Ironvarr-JOOLA(133x), Santinofuntila-JOOLA(130x), David_220cfb17-JOOLA(103x)
top_20.recommended_action: Build 'brand ambassador identification' product for equipment companies

top_20.title: Brand-skill split: PICKLEBALL KINGDOM users excel at kitchen (7.5), R&M_foz_wifi_goomba users at power (5.1)
top_20.evidence_summary: Among brands with 10+ clips: PICKLEBALL KINGDOM has highest avg kitchen mastery (7.5), while R&M_foz_wifi_goomba has highest power rating (5.1).
top_20.recommended_action: Build brand-skill correlation report for brand partnerships

top_20.title: Longitudinal tracking possible: 26 players with 5+ clips
top_20.evidence_summary: 26 players have enough data for improvement tracking. Top: PickleBill(111), Santinofuntila(63), Ironvarr(62), Tmf(59), David_220cfb17(34)
top_20.recommended_action: Build player improvement trajectory product — track skill changes over time

all_findings.title: The Brand's Own Idea Lab (White Label)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Sell Nike their own instance of our Idea Lab analyzing ONLY clips where their products appear. Nike uploads competitor videos, our system finds gaps: 'Your shoes underrepresented in backcourt angles' or 'Opponent's racket featured 3x more in viral moments.' Nike pays $200K/year, gets insights they can't get anywhere else, and becomes dependent on our data infrastructure.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: platform_play play

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Nike already has armies of sports marketing analysts and competitor tracking tools; they won't pay Pickle DaaS $50K+/year to repackage their own video library back to them.
counterargument.evidence: This assumes brands have a blind spot for their own product visibility—they don't. Nike's marketing team watches their own clips constantly and likely already uses Vision AI, Hookit, or internal tools for competitor benchmarking. More critically, you'd be asking them to hand over proprietary competitor videos to an unproven startup, creating compliance and legal risk on their end. The willingness-to-pay is near zero because the problem isn't unsolved; it's already solved by bigger, trusted vendors.

all_findings.title: Brand Bidding Arena
all_findings.evidence_summary: Flip the model: brands pay to COMPETE for shelf space in our clips. Instead of us detecting Nike in the background, Nike, JOOLA, and On bid in real-time for product placement in future Courtana videos. Facility owners get a cut. The highest bidder's gear gets featured in the next match at that court.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: monetization_novel play

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Real-time bidding for product placement in live or near-live clips requires supply (Courtana's video volume) to be predictable and brands to commit ad spend on speculative exposure.
counterargument.evidence: Courtana's clip volume is inconsistent (depends on tournament schedule). Brands won't bid on uncertain inventory. More critically, this model requires Courtana to *actively control* which products appear in clips—either by directing camera angles (unethical, undermines content credibility) or by post-processing (illegal product insertion). The model collapses if you can't guarantee placement. You'd also face immediate pushback from venue partners and athletes who won't tolerate their content being auctioned to the highest bidder.

all_findings.title: The Idea Lab as SaaS for Any Sports Facility Network
all_findings.evidence_summary: Package the Idea Lab as a $5K/month white-label product for regional sports facility chains (not just pickleball). A chain with 20 multi-sport courts gets an Idea Lab analyzing all their footage autonomously. We become the hidden OS for facility intelligence. Each facility pays us; we own the data; we expand to basketball, tennis, etc.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: platform_play play

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Regional sports facility chains won't pay $5K/month for an AI that tells them what equipment brands appear in their footage—they care about facility operations, member retention, and court booking, not brand analytics.
counterargument.evidence: You're selling a feature (brand detection) as a product to a customer (facility chains) who don't have a budget line item for brand intelligence. The buyer at a 20-court facility is the operations manager, not a marketing executive. Even if they see value, you have no distribution into this market, no existing relationships, and would need a 12-18 month sales cycle per facility. Meanwhile, you're stretched thin building this while supporting Courtana.

all_findings.title: Facility as Media Studio (Revenue Share)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Stop selling clips. Partner with facilities to become mini ESPN studios. We handle all editing/commentary/distribution; they keep 70% of ad revenue + sponsorship deals we broker. A facility with Courtana becomes a media asset. We scale to 100 facilities = 100 revenue streams. Each facility is incentivized to host bigger tournaments.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: partnership_flip play

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Facilities won't accept a 30% revenue cut and the operational overhead of becoming a 'media studio' for an unproven ad revenue share model.
counterargument.evidence: Facilities' core business is court rentals and memberships—predictable, recurring revenue. Asking them to bet on ad revenue from edited clips means cannibalizing their existing sponsorship deals (local, direct) and trusting Pickle DaaS to actually broker deals and deliver payments. You'd be taking operational risk (editing, distribution, rights management) that facilities can't control. Also: if a clip goes viral and creates liability (injury, discrimination, etc.), who owns the legal risk? This partnership structure is unresolved and will kill deals in legal review.

all_findings.title: Coaching Certification Authority
all_findings.evidence_summary: Use our AI to certify pickleball coaches. Coaches submit videos of their instruction; we analyze shot breakdown, communication clarity, drill design. Certified coaches get a badge, can charge 3x more, and must use Courtana courts to maintain certification. We become the permissioning layer for professional coaching.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: platform_play play

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?

counterargument.argument: Coaching certification requires legal liability framework, accreditation body status, and recurring instructor education—none of which Pickle DaaS is equipped to provide or defend in court.
counterargument.evidence: You cannot simply issue badges that claim to 'certify' coaches. Coaches will charge 3x more based on your certification, and if an injury occurs during a lesson from a 'certified' coach, you're exposed to negligence liability. You'd need insurance, a legal framework, appeals process, and continuing education requirements. This isn't a software feature—it's a regulated credential business. The PPA or IPTPA already own this space. You'd face immediate cease-and-desist letters, and no insurance company will cover a startup playing credential issuer.

all_findings.title: DaaS badge prediction: F1=0.97 (precision=0.98, recall=0.96)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Across 287 verified clips: 999 correct predictions, 16 false positives, 41 missed badges. Overall F1=0.972.
all_findings.recommended_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the ROI of fixing this? How many clips affected?
follow_up.proposed_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

counterargument.argument: An F1=0.97 on 287 clips doesn't prove the model works on unseen badge types or real-world production footage with different lighting, angles, or video quality.
counterargument.evidence: The 287 verified clips likely come from controlled production environments where Courtana already knows what badges exist. When the model encounters a new badge type, variation in video quality, or edge cases from user-generated content, performance will crater. The 16 false positives and 41 missed badges (out of ~1,300 total predictions) suggest systematic blindspots that prompt engineering alone won't fix—you're likely overfitting to known badge patterns.

all_findings.title: Crowdsourced Coaching Marketplace (Viral Clips → Coaching Gigs)
all_findings.evidence_summary: When a player's clip hits a viral threshold, we auto-match them with 3-5 coaches in their region. Coaches bid to coach that player. The player chooses; we take 20%. Players get better → play more matches → more clips → more coaching gigs. Each coach becomes a node in a viral loop.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: crowdsource play

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add positive examples
all_findings.evidence_summary: Production awards these but DaaS misses them: 'rally master' (6 missed), 'new look' (5 missed), 'steady eddie' (4 missed), 'fresh fit' (3 missed), 'paddle tap celebration' (3 missed). These are real badges that our prompt doesn't detect.
all_findings.recommended_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]

follow_up.follow_up_question: Can we A/B test this prompt change on 50 clips?
follow_up.proposed_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]

all_findings.title: Sponsorship whitespace: 5 equipment categories with ZERO brand detection
all_findings.evidence_summary: The camera sees these items but we detect no brands. ZERO detections: accessories, apparel, footwear, sunglasses, wristband. Near-zero: hat(5). Each gap = a brand that could be paying for visibility data.
all_findings.recommended_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock

all_findings.title: Player-Owned Highlight NFTs
all_findings.evidence_summary: Each clip becomes a fractional NFT that players, brands, and facilities can own pieces of. When a clip goes viral or gets licensed, royalties auto-distribute to all holders. Players are incentivized to play at Courtana courts (to build their highlight portfolio), facilities get ongoing micro-revenue, and brands sponsor specific players' highlight collections.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: network_effect play

all_findings.title: Net/venue branding inflates 1 brands — true share-of-court differs from raw counts
all_findings.evidence_summary: Brands with significant venue-only detections (not product use): JOOLA (195/1931 are venue). Raw counts overstate market share. Product-use only: JOOLA=1736, Nike=198, PICKLEBALL=77, HEAD=45, PICKLEBALL KINGDOM=24.
all_findings.recommended_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

follow_up.follow_up_question: Is the 'Net/venue branding inflates 1 brands — true share-' signal statistically significant with larger corpus?
follow_up.proposed_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

counterargument.argument: Correcting for venue-only detections assumes brands care about 'true share-of-court'—but they actually care about total visibility, which includes venue branding.
counterargument.evidence: Your brand partners (Nike, JOOLA) are paying for *impressions*, not just product-in-hand moments. A jersey or court net with a brand logo is still brand exposure worth money. By filtering out venue detections, you're actually reducing the value metric you're selling to them. If you report only 'product-use' metrics, you'll undercount Nike's true exposure (venue + gear) relative to competitors, and they'll ask why they're paying for an incomplete picture. You can't have it both ways: either total visibility matters (and venue counts) or you need to price models differently for each metric.

all_findings.title: Viral formula: quality (7.59 vs 5.42) + watchability (6.45 vs 3.14)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Top 100 viral clips: quality=7.59, watchability=6.45. Bottom 100: quality=5.42, watchability=3.14. Both metrics track strongly with viral potential.
all_findings.recommended_action: Use (quality + watchability) / 2 as quick viral prediction score

follow_up.follow_up_question: Can we build a predictive model from this signal?
follow_up.proposed_action: Use (quality + watchability) / 2 as quick viral prediction score

all_findings.title: Worst predicted badges: serve returner (F1=0.875), hustler (F1=0.769)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Lowest F1 badges: serve returner (F1=0.875), hustler (F1=0.769). These need prompt improvement.
all_findings.recommended_action: Add specific examples for these badge types in Gemini prompt

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the ROI of fixing this? How many clips affected?
follow_up.proposed_action: Add specific examples for these badge types in Gemini prompt

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: JOOLA appears in 215/287 verified clips — add disambiguation
all_findings.evidence_summary: JOOLA dominates 75% of clips. Prompt should distinguish: paddle logo (real JOOLA use), net logo (JOOLA sponsors nets), and venue branding (JOOLA sponsors the facility).
all_findings.recommended_action:
Add to brand detection prompt:
'For JOOLA specifically, classify detection source:
  - paddle: JOOLA logo on paddle face = confirmed product use
  - net: JOOLA logo on net = venue sponsorship, NOT product use
  - venue: JOOLA on walls/banners = facility sponsorship
Only count paddle detections as brand_category: paddle'

all_findings.title: Sponsorship whitespace detected in 973 clips (88.5%) — sell to brands
all_findings.evidence_summary: We detect unbranded equipment/space in 973 clips. This is the gap between what's visible and what's branded — sellable data.
all_findings.recommended_action: Package sponsorship whitespace data as 'brand opportunity report' for equipment companies

source_clips.summary: A 17-shot driving rally ends with an unforced error into the net.

Players engage in a consistent rally of dinks at the kitchen line.

all_findings.title: Viral shot: 'overhead' clips average 5.8 viral vs 'serve' at 1.25
all_findings.evidence_summary: Shot viral averages: overhead=5.8, volley=4.19, dink=3.22, drive=3.09, serve=1.25
all_findings.recommended_action: Prioritize highlight reels featuring high-viral shot types

all_findings.title: Viral arc: 'top_play' is 130.0x more likely in top clips (13.0% vs 0.0%)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Story arc 'top_play' appears in 13.0% of top-viral clips but only 0.0% of bottom-viral clips.
all_findings.recommended_action: Prioritize 'top_play' clips in automated highlight reels

all_findings.recommended_action: Build 'brand ambassador identification' product for equipment companies

all_findings.evidence_summary: 26 players have enough data for improvement tracking. Top: PickleBill(111), Santinofuntila(63), Ironvarr(62), Tmf(59), David_220cfb17(34)
all_findings.recommended_action: Build player improvement trajectory product — track skill changes over time

all_findings.title: Signature moves = viral gold: 1070 clips with signatures avg 3.2 viral vs 3.9
all_findings.evidence_summary: 1070 clips have identified signature moves, averaging 3.2 viral vs 3.9 for clips without.
all_findings.recommended_action: Auto-tag signature move clips as premium content

Players engage in a prolonged kitchen dink rally, demonstrating patience and control.

all_findings.title: Social caption generation at 99.8% — ready to productize
all_findings.evidence_summary: Social media captions are generated for 1098/1100 clips. This is already a viable content-as-a-service product.
all_findings.recommended_action: Create API endpoint for social caption generation — charge per clip

all_findings.title: Top content creators: PickleBill leads with 111 clips
all_findings.evidence_summary:
PickleBill: 111 clips, viral=3.3, Kitchen Master
Santinofuntila: 63 clips, viral=4.1, Dink Specialist
Ironvarr: 62 clips, viral=3.4, Kitchen Master
Tmf: 59 clips, viral=3.5, Kitchen Master

source_clips.summary: Players engage in a consistent rally of dinks at the kitchen line.
Two players engage in a consistent dink rally at the kitchen line.
Two players engage in a consistent, extended dink rally at the kitchen line.

all_findings.title: Best predicted badges: exclusive club (F1=1.0), highlight reel (F1=1.0), tag team takedown (F1=1.0)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Top performing badges by F1 score: exclusive club (F1=1.0), highlight reel (F1=1.0), tag team takedown (F1=1.0)
all_findings.recommended_action: Use these badges as confidence anchors for the prediction system

all_findings.title: Gear Insurance & Performance Guarantees
all_findings.evidence_summary: Partner with brands: 'Play with JOOLA rackets at Courtana courts and we guarantee your win rate improves by 12% in 8 weeks or money back.' We analyze your gameplay before/after. Insurance company backs the guarantee. Brands get conversion proof, players get skin in the game, we get the data AND a cut of insurance premiums.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: partnership_flip play

all_findings.title: 'strategic' is 73.1% dink — coaching archetype
all_findings.evidence_summary: The 'strategic' arc (810 clips) is dominated by dink shots (73.1%). This is a clear coaching pattern.
all_findings.recommended_action: Create 'strategic' coaching module focused on dink technique

all_findings.title: Player archetype distribution: Kitchen Master dominates (21/29 players)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Among 29 identified players: Kitchen Master: 21, Steady Eddie: 6, All-Rounder: 1, Dink Specialist: 1
all_findings.recommended_action: Build player DNA product with archetype classification as key feature

all_findings.title: Skill standout: Ajr averages 8.5 in consistency across 6 clips
all_findings.evidence_summary: Ajr skill profile: consistency=8.5, kitchen=8.2, touch=8.0, composure=7.5, court_iq=7.0, court_coverage=6.2, athleticism=5.2, power=3.0, creativity=3.0
all_findings.recommended_action: Feature Ajr in coaching product showcase

source_clips.summary: A long kitchen rally ends with a powerful overhead smash.
A lengthy kitchen rally featuring consistent dinks ends with a net error.
Players engage in a lengthy kitchen rally, ending with a net error.

all_findings.title: Tier accuracy gap: 'wood' at 100% vs 'bronze' at 94%
all_findings.evidence_summary: Badge tier prediction accuracy: wood=100%, brick=97%, silver=97%, gold=94%, bronze=94%
all_findings.recommended_action: Focus prompt improvement on bronze-tier badges

all_findings.title: Arc-specific prompt issue: 'strategic' clips cause 94% of false positives
all_findings.evidence_summary: The 'strategic' arc type produces disproportionate badge false positives (15/16 = 94%). The prompt may be misinterpreting this arc type's visual patterns.
all_findings.recommended_action: Add arc-specific badge guidance: 'For strategic clips, badge X is common but Y is not'

all_findings.title: Rally length matters: long rallies are more viral (long=3.3 vs short=2.4)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Avg rally: 16.6 shots. Long rallies (15+): 638 clips, avg viral 3.3. Short (4-): 25 clips, avg viral 2.4.
all_findings.recommended_action: Use rally length as a quality signal in highlight curation

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: Quality scores too clustered (avg=6.4, std=0.8) — increase spread
all_findings.evidence_summary: Quality scores cluster around 6.4 with std=0.8. Scores should use the full 1-10 range. Prompt may need calibration examples.
all_findings.recommended_action:
Add quality calibration to prompt:
'Quality score examples: 1-2 = unwatchable, 3-4 = poor angles/dark, 5-6 = standard gameplay, 7-8 = exciting with good camera, 9-10 = broadcast quality'

all_findings.title: Coaching feedback covers 99.8% of clips — drill library is buildable now
all_findings.evidence_summary: Coaching analysis available for 1098/1100 clips.
all_findings.recommended_action: Build 'AI Coaching Library' — categorized drills from real game footage

all_findings.title: 'dink' players skew kitchen: power=3.2 vs kitchen=7.5
all_findings.evidence_summary: Players using dink as dominant shot (748 clips) average power=3.2 and kitchen=7.5.
all_findings.recommended_action: Use shot type as player skill segmentation signal

all_findings.title: Viral rally length: top clips avg 17 shots vs bottom 13
all_findings.evidence_summary: Top-viral clips average 17.1 shots per rally, bottom-viral average 13.1.
all_findings.recommended_action: Investigate optimal rally length range for highlight reels

all_findings.title: Viral skill bias: kitchen = 7.5 in viral vs 5.6 in non-viral clips
all_findings.evidence_summary: Viral clips show kitchen average of 7.5 vs 5.6 in low-viral clips.
all_findings.recommended_action: Consider kitchen as a viral prediction feature

all_findings.title: Emerging brands detected: 35 brands with 2-8 appearances
all_findings.evidence_summary: Rare but real brand detections: goomba (8), R&M (7), Pickleball (7), Onix (6), Reolink (6), Foz Bullet UG 4K (6), PBS (5), adidas (4). These represent market entrants or niche brands.
all_findings.recommended_action: Monitor emerging brand frequency over next 500 clips for trend confirmation

all_findings.title: Extraction gap: 'player_identity' only fills 28.3% of clips — low-hanging improvement
all_findings.evidence_summary: Current fill rates: player_identity=28.3%, brands=80.5%, sponsorship_whitespace=88.5%, espn_commentary=91.5%, hype_commentary=91.5%, signature_move=97.3%, play_style=99.6%, coaching=99.8%, social_caption=99.8%
all_findings.recommended_action: Improve Gemini prompt for 'player_identity' extraction with more examples
